questions.question: Why does Stable Diffusion run its DDPM in a 4×64×64 latent space rather than directly on 3×512×512 pixel images?
options: Latents are the native output of GPUs
To make inference deterministic

Training and sampling on 16,384 latent values instead of 786,432 pixels is roughly 48× cheaper; a pretrained VAE handles the image ↔ latent conversion, and the diffusion model only has to model the structured latent manifold
Latents are easier to visualise than pixels
questions.explanation: Latent diffusion is the single innovation that made consumer-GPU text-to-image practical. The VAE compresses spatial and channel information by 48×. The diffusion model spends its entire parameter budget modelling the latent manifold — the part the user sees after decoding.

questions.question: In the Stable Diffusion U-Net, what role does cross-attention serve at every spatial resolution?
options: It attends from latent tokens to text-embedding tokens, injecting prompt information into the denoising process at every resolution level of the encoder and decoder
It attends between adjacent timesteps to improve temporal coherence across sampling steps
It replaces self-attention to reduce the total parameter count below 860M
It is applied only at the bottleneck layer to keep the skip-connection shapes consistent
questions.explanation: Cross-attention in the SD U-Net connects latent feature tokens (query) to the CLIP/T5 text-embedding sequence (key, value). Placing it at every resolution — not just the bottleneck — ensures the prompt steers both coarse structure and fine texture. This is why swapping the text encoder (CLIP-L → T5) changes generation quality without retraining the U-Net weights.

questions.question: In `StableDiffusionImg2ImgPipeline`, what does the `strength` parameter control, and why does `strength=1.0` ignore the input image entirely?
options: The guidance scale applied to the text prompt; higher values mean stronger prompt adherence

The fraction of total denoising steps applied to the encoded latent: at strength=1.0 the pipeline adds full Gaussian noise to the encoded latent before denoising, erasing all original content; at 0.0 no noise is added and the image is unchanged
The resolution scaling factor applied before encoding the image into the VAE
The learning rate used during the LoRA adapter merge step
questions.explanation: `strength` controls how much noise is injected into the encoded latent before denoising begins. At 0.0 the pipeline runs zero steps and returns the original; at 1.0 it adds noise equivalent to starting from pure noise, so the input image has no effect. The range 0.5–0.7 is the sweet spot for style transfer: structure survives encoding, and the prompt reshapes appearance.

questions.question: LoRA injects the update W_q + α·(A @ B) into each attention weight. If d_in=1024, d_out=1024, and rank r=16, what are the shapes of A and B, and which tensors receive gradient?
options: A: (1024, 1024), B: (1024, 1024); both receive gradient — LoRA is identical to full fine-tuning at low rank
A: (1024, 16), B: (16, 1024); W_q receives gradient while A and B are frozen
A: (16, 16), B: (16, 16); all three matrices are trained jointly

A: (1024, 16), B: (16, 1024); only A and B receive gradient while the base W_q remains frozen — the total trainable parameter count for this layer is 1024×16 + 16×1024 = 32,768 vs 1,048,576 for full fine-tuning
questions.explanation: LoRA's efficiency comes from the rank factorisation: A maps from d_in to r, B maps from r to d_out. With r=16 and d=1024, each layer has 32,768 trainable parameters instead of 1M+. The base W_q is frozen — no gradient flows through it. At inference, fusing W_q + α·(A @ B) produces a single matrix with no extra latency.

questions.question: You swap SD's default scheduler for DPM-Solver++ 2M Karras and reduce `num_inference_steps` from 50 to 20. What is the expected result, and does the U-Net need retraining?
options: Comparable image quality in roughly half the compute; DPM-Solver++ is a second-order ODE integrator that converges in fewer steps than DDIM for the same sample quality, and schedulers are fully decoupled from U-Net weights — no retraining required
Worse quality and longer runtime because the scheduler change invalidates the learned noise schedule
Numerical instability causing NaN outputs after 10 steps
You must retrain the U-Net on the new solver's timestep scheme before it produces coherent images
questions.explanation: Schedulers are decoupled from model weights. DPM-Solver++ is a higher-order ODE solver that achieves DDIM-50 quality in about 20 steps. Zero retraining required, and it is the production default in 2026. Going below 8 steps typically needs distilled or consistency-model variants like LCM or Turbo.

questions.question: Why is LoRA fine-tuning the dominant distribution format for Stable Diffusion community fine-tunes instead of full checkpoints?
options: LoRA produces higher-quality images than full fine-tuning by default

LoRA keeps the 860M base U-Net frozen and inserts small rank-decomposition matrices into attention layers, so a fine-tune trains in minutes on consumer hardware, produces 10–50 MB adapters, and can be swapped or combined at inference without touching the base checkpoint
LoRA is required by the `diffusers` library API and cannot be bypassed
LoRA prevents overfitting entirely, making dataset curation unnecessary
questions.explanation: LoRA's value is training cost and distribution efficiency. Full SD fine-tuning updates 860M+ parameters and needs 20+ GB of VRAM. LoRA updates ~1–10M parameters and fits in 6–8 GB. The base model is unchanged, so the same LoRA loads into any compatible checkpoint. Multiple LoRAs can be combined with weighted merging. CivitAI's entire ecosystem is built on LoRAs.
